title: Rabdosia henryi
intro: Rabdosia henryi, also known as San-Huang-Xi, is a species of flowering plant in the mint family (Lamiaceae) native to the mountainous regions of China. Traditionally, Rabdosia henryi has been used in herbal medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory infections, due to its anti-inflammatory, antiviral, and immunomodulatory properties. The bioactive constituents of Rabdosia henryi include diterpenoids, flavonoids, and alkaloids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional Chinese medicine, Rabdosia henryi is often prepared as a decoction or dried powder, and is commonly used to make herbal formulas such as San-Huang-Xi decoction and San-Huang-Xi powder.
taxonomy: Rabdosia henryi is a species of flowering plant in the family Lamiaceae. It is native to the mountainous regions of southwestern China. The genus Rabdosia is classified under the subfamily Lamioideae, which is a group of plants commonly known as the "deadnettle alliance." This subfamily is part of the larger family Lamiaceae, which includes a wide range of plants such as mint, basil, and oregano. Rabdosia henryi is a rare and endangered species due to habitat loss and overcollection.

Its medicinal properties include anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, which have been studied for potential applications in treating various health conditions. Some studies suggest that Rabdosia henryi may have immunomodulatory and anticancer properties, although more research is needed to confirm these findings.
